title: JIT wire-up 1 of 2 — profiler at the call edges, threshold-tripped compilation through the composed dirty-CPU service
purpose: Ledger A1, first brief (docs/stack-review/beamr-ledger.md §A1 — 'highest payoff-per-brief in the repo'). Both interpreter call edges ALREADY consult the JIT cache and run native code on a hit (dispatch_local_jit, opcodes/core.rs:639; dispatch_external_jit, opcodes/core.rs:704 — generation-keyed via module.generation(), invalidation machinery landed). The profiler ALREADY implements the whole hotness protocol (record_call with INTERPRETING/PENDING/COMPILED/UNSUPPORTED states, profiler.rs:113). The compilation job is ALREADY self-contained (compile → cache.insert + mark_compiled; unsupported → mark_unsupported; transient → reset_counter; compile_job.rs:60-85) and ALREADY submits through the composed dirty-CPU ServiceMode with a typed disabled-refusal (try_submit_jit_compilation, compile_job.rs:104). What does not exist is the wire between them: nothing calls record_call, so no job is ever submitted and the headline performance feature is dormant. This brief closes exactly that gap and nothing else. LEDGER-DRIFT NOTE (binding): the ledger's A-tier is stated against v0.12.0, pre-composition. This brief lands on the composed surface: submission rides the dirty-CPU ServiceMode and the answer to 'which compositions carry a live profiler' reuses the OQ-1 reasoning of record verbatim (docs/READINESS-REGISTRATION-API.md §8 OQ-1): jit stays FEATURE-COMPILED exactly as today; the PROFILER is SERVICE-ENABLED — live only where the dirty-CPU service is live and never under replay. Base: beamr main at 0.14.0 (eeb1dd1 or later).
task: Wire JitProfiler::record_call into the two bytecode-fallthrough paths at the interpreter's call edges, and on RecordResult::CompileNow assemble a CompilationRequest for the function's instruction slice at the module's current generation and submit it via try_submit_jit_compilation. Plumb the profiler and submission handle along the same seam jit_cache travels today (build_native_services → NativeServices → interpreter contexts), gated at the construction site on replay mode and dirty-CPU service liveness. Add compile-outcome counters exposed through the existing telemetry surface. Extend the differential harness to prove interpreter/JIT equivalence through the WIRED scheduler path, and pin the replay and disabled-composition refusals. Deviations from this brief — including improvements — STOP and report; they are not improvised (house rule, composition-campaign precedent).

requirements.title: Profiler reachability — same seam as jit_cache, gated at construction
requirements.spec: Add the profiler handle (and whatever submission handle R3 needs) to NativeServices beside its existing jit_cache field (interpreter/mod.rs:106) and thread it into the interpreter's dispatch contexts beside jit_cache (JitDispatchContext / ExtCallContext, opcodes/core.rs:36,47). Populate it in build_native_services (supervision_integration.rs:635, where jit_cache is cloned from shared today) — that site holds all three gating facts. The handle is Some IFF: (a) the jit feature is compiled (reuse the existing cfg structure — no new cfg patterns); (b) shared.replay_mode is false — replay composes the profiler AWAY entirely, commit-5 precedent (replay validates reduction counts; JIT charges differ, ledger §A1); (c) the dirty-CPU service is live (ServiceMode not Disabled). When the handle is None, record_call is unreachable and the call edges bear zero added cost — absence is the disable mechanism, not a runtime flag check inside the edge.
acceptance: Default profile (from_config, dirty-CPU live): profiler handle present at both edges
Replay mode with jit feature compiled: handle absent; a replayed program's outputs are byte-identical to pre-brief behavior and no compilation is ever submitted
SchedulerServices::minimal() (dirty-CPU Disabled): handle absent; R4's counters prove zero submission attempts
No new #[allow]; no runtime branch on replay/service state inside the dispatch functions themselves

requirements.title: record_call on the bytecode fall-through at both call edges — GENERATION-STAMPED (reviewer condition T1)
requirements.spec: In dispatch_local_jit (opcodes/core.rs:639) and dispatch_external_jit (opcodes/core.rs:704): today a cache miss falls through to Ok(None) → bytecode. On that MISS path only — a cache hit must add no work, so record after the lookup misses, not before — call record_call with the same identity the lookup used PLUS the generation it used: (module.name, function, arity, module.generation()). GENERATION SEMANTICS (T1, verbatim-or-better fold): record_call's signature gains the generation; FunctionProfile stamps the generation it was heated at; when a call arrives with a NEWER generation than the stamp, the profile atomically resets to INTERPRETING at the new generation with a zeroed counter BEFORE counting — a fresh generation is a fresh function, so previously-COMPILED functions re-heat and recompile after a hot load, and previously-UNSUPPORTED functions retry (their code changed; the old verdict is about code that no longer runs). Without this, the generation-keyed cache (cache.rs) and the generation-blind profiler state machine (profiler.rs:113-122) compose into permanent demotion: compile at gen N → hot-load N+1 → cache misses forever on N+1 while the profiler answers COMPILED forever — every hot reload demotes exactly the hot functions on the platform whose selling point is hot reload, and Frame's component remove/reinstall lifecycle makes that the common path, not the edge. RecordResult::Continue proceeds to bytecode unchanged. MIXED-GENERATION CLAUSE (reviewer closing clause, option (a) adopted): calls whose generation is OLDER than the profile's stamp neither reset nor count — they return Continue untouched. This closes the PENDING wedge: without it, an old-generation call could be the threshold-crosser, submit a job at ITS generation N, and the completion's mark_compiled(N) would NO-OP against the N+1 stamp (R3), stranding the profile in PENDING — which record_call treats as terminal, so N+1 never re-heats: the demotion returning through the door built to kill it. Under (a) the threshold-crossing call is BY CONSTRUCTION at the stamp's generation, so the completion mark always matches the stamp, and the counter's meaning stays clean — one profile counts one generation's heat. The named honest cost: a long-lived old-generation process's hot code simply never JITs after a newer generation loads (old code is dying; bounded by that process's remaining lifetime). Do not special-case compiled/unsupported functions at the edge: the (now generation-scoped) state machine returns Continue for them within a generation, and once the cache is populated the lookup hits so the edge stops recording naturally. The existing test enshrining terminal COMPILED (mark_compiled_prevents_retriggering) is updated to be generation-scoped: same generation still prevents retriggering; a newer generation does not.
acceptance: Unit test: threshold-1 calls to a supported function produce zero submissions; the threshold-th call produces exactly one (threshold from SchedulerConfig.jit_threshold, honoring the existing DEFAULT_JIT_THRESHOLD fallback at scheduler/mod.rs:1039-1040)
After compilation lands in the cache, subsequent calls dispatch native and the profile counter stops moving (record-on-miss-only, observed via profiler state)
T1 acceptance: hot-load v2 of a previously-compiled function re-heats, recompiles, and dispatches native at the new generation (end-to-end through the wired path)
T1 acceptance: a previously-UNSUPPORTED function retries at the new generation (profiler state observed INTERPRETING after the first new-generation call)

Mixed-generation acceptance (closing clause): with an old-generation process still calling the function while the new generation heats, the NEW generation compiles and dispatches native — 10x stable; the old-generation calls are proven not to count (counter unmoved by them)
Within one generation, COMPILED and UNSUPPORTED remain terminal (the updated mark_compiled_prevents_retriggering pin)
Both edges covered by tests — local and external call forms

requirements.title: CompileNow — non-blocking submission through the composed service, completion marks generation-scoped
requirements.spec: On RecordResult::CompileNow, assemble CompilationRequest::new(module, function, arity, module.generation(), instructions) where instructions is EXACTLY the target function's slice of the loaded module's instruction stream, obtained via Module::function_instructions (R8 — specified after the build leg's correct STOP-AND-REPORT on the missing accessor; never inline slicing logic at the call edge). Submit CompilationJob::new(request, compiler, profiler, cache) via try_submit_jit_compilation (compile_job.rs:104). GENERATION SCOPING OF COMPLETION MARKS (T1's race variant, closed structurally): mark_compiled, mark_unsupported, and reset_counter gain the generation parameter and NO-OP when the profile's stamped generation is newer — CompilationJob already holds the exact generation it compiled (JitCacheKey, compile_job.rs:29) and passes key.generation. Without this, a job completing for generation N after N+1 has loaded stamps COMPILED onto the N+1 profile whose cache entry doesn't exist — the same permanent demotion T1 names, arriving through the completion race instead of the load. The compiler is one Arc<JitCompiler> per scheduler, constructed once at SharedState build beside jit_profiler (scheduler/mod.rs:1039); JitCompiler::new returns Result — a construction failure in a jit-enabled build propagates out of the Scheduler constructor as an error (fatal-loud, the readiness ReadinessBuildError precedent; never expect/unwrap, never silent degradation). Submission is fire-and-forget from the calling process's perspective: it never blocks, and NO submission failure surfaces into BEAM semantics — Err(Disabled) is unreachable by R1's gating but if raced, and Err on a stopped/full pool, call profiler.reset_counter for the MFA at the submitted generation (transient; the function can re-heat) and continue interpreting.
acceptance: A hot function's compilation job runs on a dirty-CPU worker and its result reaches the cache: subsequent calls observably dispatch native (differential path in R5 consumes this)

Submission-failure path pinned: a refused/failed submission leaves the calling process running bytecode with no error and the profile counter reset (fail-first: verify the pin fails if reset_counter is dropped)

Scheduler construction with jit feature and a failing JitCompiler::new surfaces the error from the constructor (may be pinned via cfg(test) seam if cranelift cannot be made to fail naturally; if no honest seam exists, STOP and report)

requirements.title: Compile-outcome counters on the telemetry surface
requirements.spec: Count submissions, compile successes, unsupported markings, and transient failures. The counting seam is the builder's choice within existing patterns (plain AtomicU64s on JitProfiler read by the telemetry layer fits the house instrument style; TelemetryMetricState at scheduler/mod.rs is the exposure pattern) — but if the choice requires NEW cross-crate machinery, STOP and report. Counters must be cheap enough to live outside cfg(telemetry) (atomic increments), with only the EXPOSURE gated on the telemetry feature, so the R5 pins can consume them in default builds.
acceptance: Counters move correctly under R5's hot-path test (1 submission, 1 success for a supported function; unsupported path exercised via a function the JIT tier rejects)
Replay and minimal() compositions: all counters remain zero across the same program
cargo clippy --all-features clean (telemetry feature exercised)

requirements.title: Differential acceptance on the wired path + refusal pins
requirements.spec: The existing differential harness (tests/differential.rs) proves interpreter-vs-JIT equivalence by driving both directly; it does NOT exercise the wire. Add scheduler-driven coverage: a program calls a JIT-supported function (threshold + K) times THROUGH the scheduler, the test waits for the compilation to land (bounded wait on the R4 success counter — never a bare sleep), and asserts the post-compile results are equal to the same program's results with the profiler absent (minimal-composition run of identical code). Whether this extends differential.rs or lands as a sibling integration binary is the builder's structural call — matching the harness's existing proptest shape matters less than consuming ONLY public paths (first-external-consumer gate, now standing doctrine). Two refusal pins ride with it: (a) the same program under replay submits nothing and its outputs match the pre-brief interpreter exactly; (b) under minimal() the program runs interpreter-only with zero submissions and identical results.
acceptance: Wired-path equivalence test green and 10x stable in isolation
Replay pin and minimal() pin both green, both counter-verified (zero submissions)
All three tests consume public API only
Fail-first evidence for the equivalence test: with record_call wiring reverted, the test detects the missing compilation (bounded wait expires) — recorded in the handoff, not committed

requirements.title: Module::function_instructions — the per-function slice accessor (resolves the build leg's STOP-AND-REPORT)
requirements.spec: The build leg stopped exactly where R3 told it to: the loaded Module type has no per-function instruction-slice accessor (function_at_ip returns identity only, module.rs:176; the only slicing logic in the tree is aot::exported_instructions at aot.rs:337-362, which operates on ParsedModule, not Module). The accessor is hereby specified rather than improvised: add `Module::function_instructions(&self, entry_ip: usize) -> Option<&[Instruction]>` in module.rs beside function_at_ip. SEMANTICS MIRROR THE AOT SLICER EXACTLY, because JitCompiler::compile already consumes slices of that shape from both the AOT path and the differential harness: start at entry_ip, advance past the single entry Label/FuncInfo prelude instruction exactly as exported_instructions does (one conditional skip, not a loop); end before the next FuncInfo instruction or at code end. The end boundary MAY use the sorted function_table (it IS the func_info table, module.rs:119) instead of a linear scan, PROVIDED the produced slice is element-identical to the AOT slicer's. Returns None when entry_ip is outside the code or no function contains it. Rustdoc states the contract: 'the slice shape JitCompiler::compile consumes — identical to aot::exported_instructions for the same function.'
acceptance: Slice-equality pin: for EVERY export of a fixture module, Module::function_instructions on the loaded module yields an element-identical slice to aot::exported_instructions on the parsed module (this is the wall that keeps the two slicers from drifting; it must FAIL if either changes shape unilaterally)
None for an ip outside code bounds and for an ip before the first function entry
The R3 submission path assembles CompilationRequest from this accessor at the calling module's generation

requirements.title: Profile lifecycle across module delete — bounded growth, named (reviewer condition T1, lifecycle half)
requirements.spec: Two mechanisms and one honestly-named bound. (1) SELF-HEALING AT THE EDGE: R2's generation stamping means any profile whose module reloads is reset on the next call — no load-seam callback is needed, and registry mutations that bypass the scheduler entirely (embedders holding the Arc<ModuleRegistry> directly) are covered by the same mechanism, because the edge reads module.generation() from the module it is actually about to run. (2) DELETE SEAM: the scheduler-mediated module delete path (ModuleRegistry::delete_module, module.rs:412 — wire at the scheduler's module-management call site, NOT inside the module crate; the module crate must not learn about the profiler) drops all profile entries for the deleted module via a new JitProfiler::remove_module(module: Atom). (3) THE NAMED BOUND: entries for modules deleted WITHOUT the scheduler seam (raw-registry embedders) linger until process end; growth is bounded by the count of distinct MFAs ever recorded at an edge — each entry is one DashMap slot with two atomics, and an MFA only enters the map by being interpreted at a live call edge. This bound is stated in the profiler's rustdoc where the map is declared. Silence on lifecycle was the reviewer's only-wrong-answer; this requirement is the non-silence.
acceptance: delete_module through the scheduler path drops the module's profile entries (observed via a test-support count or profiler state probe)
The growth bound and its raw-registry caveat are in the profiler rustdoc, stated as a bound (what grows, what bounds it), not a disclaimer
A reload-without-delete cycle (the common hot-load) leaves exactly one entry per MFA — generation stamping reuses the slot, it does not accumulate per-generation entries

requirements.title: Full gate bar on the final head
requirements.spec: The composition-campaign bar, unchanged: cargo fmt --check; cargo check -p beamr; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings AND --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -p beamr --lib; cargo test -p beamr --test '*'; 10x stability loops on every new pin. Zero new #[allow] attributes anywhere, including #[allow(deprecated)]. No unwrap/expect/panic outside tests. Handoff pack states toolchain versions (rustc + clippy) measured on the final head.
acceptance: All gates exit 0 on the final head, toolchain-stamped
grep proves zero new #[allow] in the diff

boundaries: A1-2 (CLI .beamr_native companion loading) is OUT — its own future brief, numbered when it earns a timeline (coordinator's numbering ruling 2026-07-22: brief numbers record dispatch order; JIT-002 went to the coverage lane)
A2 lowerings are OUT: no changes to the compiler, IR modules, or supported-opcode set; if the differential test needs a 'JIT-supported function', pick one the current tier already compiles
tune_threshold exists but stays unwired — adaptive thresholds are not this brief
No changes to replay semantics beyond the R1 gating; no changes to dirty-pool machinery, teardown admissions, or ServiceMode arms — this brief CONSUMES the composed surface, it does not extend it
No new services, no inventory changes: the profiler is not a service and must not grow an inventory line (it owns no threads; compilation runs on the dirty-CPU pool's already-inventoried workers)
verification: Full R6 bar on the final head. R5's three tests are the acceptance spine: wired-path equivalence (10x), replay refusal pin, minimal-composition refusal pin — each fail-first verified where the spec names it. Reviewer-of-record per house convention; norn adversarial rounds ride in addition to the bar as the campaign established.
